title: Correlated $\mathcal{PT}$-Symmetric Antiferromagnetic Topological Insulators with Giant Nonlinear Anomalous Thermoelectrics
authors: Heng-Yu Di, Zhen-Gang Zhu, Gang Su
abstract: Topological states in antiferromagnets (AFMs) offer a promising platform for exploring novel physical phenomena and advancing the applications of AFM spintronics. The AFM topological insulator (TI) state stands out as one of the most representative and prominent cases. Unlike the previously proposed AFM-TI states in noninteracting systems, here we employ an extended Kane-Mele-Hubbard model to demonstrate that electron correlations can give rise to a $\mathcal{PT}$-symmetric AFM-TI state. This state breaks both spatial inversion symmetry $\mathcal{P}$ and time-reversal symmetry $\mathcal{T}$, and enables intrinsic topological nonlinear responses to dominate the leading-order dynamics of the system. The competition between electron correlations and spin-orbit coupling drives the system across a topological phase transition, where the closure of the bulk band gap induces singular behaviors in higher-order quantum geometric tensors. Such microscopic singular characteristics manifest macroscopically as pronounced enhancements in thermoelectric performance, charge conductivity, and thermal conductivity. These giant tunable transport signatures, which can be effectively modulated by mechanical strain and electrostatic gating, provide a feasible experimental route to probe and understand correlated topological materials.
summary: This work theoretically explores correlated Antiferromagnetic Topological Insulators using an extended Kane-Mele-Hubbard model. It demonstrates that electron correlations stabilize a unique $\mathcal{PT}$-symmetric phase, and critically, the topological phase transition within this system causes a giant enhancement in nonlinear anomalous thermoelectric responses. The results show these tunable transport signatures can be controlled by external stimuli like strain or gating.
main_problem: To investigate novel physical phenomena in Antiferromagnetic (AFM) spintronics by studying correlated topological states that exhibit giant nonlinear anomalous thermoelectric responses.
main_result: Electron correlations stabilize a $\mathcal{PT}$-symmetric AFM-TI phase, and the topological phase transition at gap closure leads to a giant enhancement of all nonlinear anomalous transport coefficients.
method: The study uses theoretical modeling based on an extended Kane-Mele-Hubbard model, employing the Hartree-Fock mean-field approximation for correlation effects.
model_or_system: The system is modeled by an extended Kane-Mele-Hubbard Hamiltonian on a honeycomb lattice, incorporating hopping terms, SOC ($\lambda$), and the Hubbard interaction ($U$). The analysis focuses on the transition between Semimetal (SM), Topological Insulator (TI), AFM-TI, and trivial AFM phases.
key_observables: Nonlinear anomalous electrical ($\sigma_{abc}$), thermoelectric ($\alpha_{abc}$), and thermal ($\kappa_{abc}$) conductivities; topological invariants like the Chern number ($C$).
important_parameters: On-site Coulomb interaction ($U$), SOC strength ($\lambda$), uniaxial strain ($\eta_1$), and electron filling ($n$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on the Hartree-Fock mean-field approximation for treating electron correlations, and linear anomalous conductivities vanish in $\mathcal{PT}$-symmetric AFM-TIs.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate phase diagrams in $(U, \lambda)$ space showing different topological phases; band structures across these regimes; and plots demonstrating the sharp peaks of nonlinear anomalous conductivities at the critical point ($U=U_c$).
paper_structure: The paper systematically analyzes the electronic structure through various correlated magnetic phases (TI $ightarrow$ AFM-TI $ightarrow$ AFM), identifying the topological phase transition via gap closure, and then calculating the resulting giant enhancements in nonlinear transport responses.
why_interesting: While focused on condensed matter magnetism, the study of symmetry breaking ($\mathcal{PT}$-symmetry) and emergent non-linear response tensors driven by band topology provides theoretical frameworks relevant to open quantum systems dynamics and engineered material responses.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Phase diagram and topological transition in the extended Kane-Mele-Hubbard model. (a) Ground- state phase diagram in the (U, λ) parameter space. Parameters are set as: η1 = 1.05, nA = nB = 1.005, t2 = 0.1t, and kBT = 0.01t. Four phases are identified: Semimetal (SM), Topological Insulator (TI), Antiferromagnetic TI (AFM-TI), and trivial Antiferromagnetic Insulator (AFM). The magnetic phase transition from TI to AFM-TI is marked by the onset of a nonzero local magnetization (m ̸= 0), while the topological transition from AFM-TI phase to AFM phase is determined by the bulk gap closing (∆= 0). Inset: Honeycomb lattice schematic with modified bond vectors δi. (b) Band structures across...

FIG. 2. Microscopic origin and momentum-space geo- metric evolution. Distributions of (a) the Berry curvature (Ωyx), (b) the qy-even components of the BCP dipole (Λyxx even), and (c) the TBCP dipole (Λyxx,t even ) near the Dirac valley K+. The four columns illustrate the evolution across four distinct regimes at a fixed λ = 0.05t. The yellow dot in the third column marks the gapless Dirac point where the geometric quantities diverge.

FIG. 3. Giant nonlinear anomalous responses at the topological critical point. Calculated non-zero compo- nents of the nonlinear anomalous electrical (σyxx), thermo- electric (αyxx), and thermal (κyxx) conductivities as a func- tion of the correlation U. All coefficients are normalized by their respective absolute maximum values, with the sign of κyxx inverted for visual comparison.

FIG. 4. Tunability of the topological phase transi- tions. Evolution of the band Chern number C, which de- marcates the topological transition of the AFM-TI phase, as a function of (a) the strain parameter η1 and (b) the electron filling n = nA = nB for various interaction strengths U.
